source.title: Same or different? ERP correlates of pretense and false belief reasoning in children

confidence_rationale: ERP study showing that pretense reasoning and false belief reasoning recruit distinct neural processes in 6-8 year olds: pretense elicits an early parietal P2 (incongruence detection) and negative frontal slow wave (intention processing); false belief elicits the characteristic late positive fronto-central slow wave (metarepresentation). This challenges the strong Leslie account of pretend play and false belief sharing the same neural substrate. For REE, this is mixed evidence: it supports that the play-frame tag and the hypothesis tag are distinct cognitive operations (consistent with ARC-049 + MECH-094 being separate mechanisms), but conflicts with a simple 'pretend play exercises false-belief architecture' story. The study uses 6-8 year olds (past the pretend play acquisition window) and ERP is indirect measure of neural substrate.
failure_signatures: If pretend play and false belief shared the same neurocognitive substrate, ERP components should be identical in polarity, latency, and topography -- the observed differences (early/parietal/negative for pretense vs late/fronto-central/positive for FB) argue against substrate identity.

The negative frontal slow wave for pretense reasoning is attributed to 'intention processing' (simpler mentalistic process) rather than full metarepresentation -- suggesting children process others' pretense through behavioral-intentional cues without necessarily engaging the full hypothesis_tag+play_frame system.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In 6-8 year old children, pretense reasoning and false belief reasoning produce distinct ERP signatures in timing, polarity, and scalp distribution, suggesting they rely on distinct neurocognitive mechanisms; pretense may use simpler intention-processing rather than full metarepresentation.
mapping.ree_translation: This is consistent with REE's two-tag decomposition: ARC-049 (bilateral play frame, intention-level tagging) and MECH-094 (hypothesis tag, full metarepresentational decoupling) are distinct mechanisms that co-operate during pretend play but are individually separable. Pretense reasoning in 6-8 year olds may rely primarily on ARC-049 (frame recognition from behavioral/intentional cues) without necessarily recruiting the full MECH-094 hypothesis-tag system. This is a refining finding for REE, not a contradicting one -- REE's two-tag architecture predicts exactly this dissociation.
mapping.mapping_caveat: ERP components are temporally precise but spatially coarse. The 6-8 year age range is past the pretend play acquisition window; whether these distinct components reflect the same dissociation that exists at 18-36 months (when pretend play emerges) is unknown. The task is *reasoning about* pretense, not naturalistic pretend play production.
mapping.source_context: N=28 children, ages 6-8, German lab. Within-subject ERP design. Pretense condition, false belief condition, non-mental control condition.

confidence_components.notes: Good source: Neuroscience journal, ERP methodology, controlled design. Moderate mapping fidelity: neural dissociation supports two-tag architecture but the specific component-to-mechanism mapping is inferential. Moderate transfer risk: task is reasoning about others' pretense (not production), age range is past acquisition window.
